history.author: Hassam Nasir
history.titleZh: 消息称英伟达正在研发搭载9GB显存的RTX 5050显卡，采用96位总线与28 Gbps GDDR7显存模块，同时基于精简版GB205 GPU的RTX 5060也正在规划中。
history.descriptionZh:
据知名硬件泄露者@Zed__Wang的信息，全球零部件短缺问题正在深刻影响英伟达的产品规划。作为这场缺货危机的主要推动者之一，英伟达的消费级显卡市场同样受到冲击，迫使公司考虑推出一些非常规的新产品。

根据泄露，英伟达可能正在研发一款新的GeForce RTX 5050显卡，其显存配置颇为独特：将搭载9GB GDDR7显存，显存总线位宽则从当前RTX 5050的128位削减至96位。该显卡将采用28 Gbps速率的GDDR7芯片，取代现有的20 Gbps GDDR6模块。这一变化使得显存带宽从320 GB/s略微提升至336 GB/s，增幅约为5%。虽然显存容量从8GB增加到了9GB，但位宽的缩减限制了带宽的显著增长。该泄露者指出，英伟达本可以使用新的3GB GDDR7芯片打造128位位宽、12GB显存的RTX 5050/5060，但最终可能选择了目前的方案。

在核心规格上，预计新款RTX 5050将沿用GB207 GPU芯片，基于台积电5nm工艺，拥有2560个CUDA核心，热设计功耗为130W。时钟频率的细节有待产品发布时确认。值得注意的是，96位显存接口在英伟达桌面GPU中并非首次出现，2024年的RTX 3050就曾采用过。近期RTX 5050和5060是少数价格保持相对稳定的显卡，涨幅仅在10美元左右。

与此同时，泄露信息也涉及了RTX 5060的产品路线调整。由于英伟达已告知合作厂商（AIB）专注于生产搭载8GB显存的RTX 5060 Ti，导致用于标准版RTX 5060的GB206芯片供应短缺。为解决这一问题，英伟达可能被迫转向使用降级的GB205芯片来生产RTX 5060。GB205本是用于更高端的RTX 5070的芯片。

这一调整意味着：
1.  **芯片降级利用**：有缺陷或降级的GB205芯片将被重新利用。
2.  **核心数量大幅削减**：GB205芯片原生拥有6144个CUDA核心，为适配RTX 5060的定位，核心数将削减至3840个。
3.  **显存位宽缩减**：其显存位宽将从192位降至128位。
4.  **PCB重新设计**：合作厂商需要为RTX 5060设计新的PCB电路板，以容纳尺寸不同的GB205芯片，并可能改用传统的8-pin电源接口（RTX 5070使用的是12V-2x6接口）。

**技术影响与应用场景分析：**

这些产品变动清晰地反映了全球半导体供应链，特别是显存与特定GPU芯片的供应限制，仍在持续影响显卡市场的产品布局。英伟达的应对策略显示出其在元件短缺下的灵活（或被迫）调整：
*   **RTX 5050的9GB/96位配置**：是一种在成本、可用性和规格之间寻求平衡的折中方案。虽然GDDR7带来了速度和能效提升，但受限的位宽可能制约其在更高分辨率下的性能表现，使其更明确地定位在1080p主流游戏市场。
*   **RTX 5060的芯片替换**：则是一种典型的供应链应急方案。利用更高端芯片的降级品，可以在不中断产品线的情况下维持出货，但可能导致芯片成本结构与产品定位不匹配，同时也增加了合作厂商的设计和生产复杂度。

总体而言，这些传闻中的产品调整并非纯粹的技术革新，更多的是市场环境下的适应性策略。对于消费者而言，这意味着即将到来的中端显卡可能具备非常规的规格组合，在选购时需要更加仔细地审视其核心配置（尤其是显存位宽与带宽）与实际性能的对应关系，而非仅仅关注显存容量和代际。

history.title: Seven tech giants signed Trump’s pledge to keep electricity costs from spiking around data centers
history.description:
Trump summoned tech leaders to the White House on Wednesday, March 4, 2026 to sign pledges committing their companies to foot the electricity bill for energy-hungry data centers.  | Photo: Getty Images	

Leaders from Google, Meta, Microsoft, Oracle, OpenAI, Amazon, and xAI met with President Donald Trump today to sign a "rate payer protection pledge." It's one way they're responding to growing bipartisan concerns about electricity rates rising as tech companies and the Trump administration rush to build out a new generation of AI data centers. 
"[Tech companies] need some PR help because people think that if a data center goes in, their electricity prices are going to go up," Trump said during the event. "Some centers were rejected by communities for that and now I think it's going to be the opposite."
Trump signed a proclamation formally  …
Read the full story at The Verge.
history.content: Leaders from Google, Meta, Microsoft, Oracle, OpenAI, Amazon, and xAI met with President Donald Trump today to sign a “rate payer protection pledge.” It’s one way they’re responding to growing bipartisan concerns about electricity rates rising as tech companies and the Trump administration rush to build out a new generation of AI data centers.“[Tech companies] need some PR help because people think that if a data center goes in, their electricity prices are going to go up,” Trump said during the event. “Some centers were rejected by communities for that and now I think it’s going to be the opposite.”Trump signed a proclamation formally introducing the ratepayer protection pledge today during a roundtable event, following up on claims he made during his State of the Union speech last week. The proclamation says that “these companies will build, bring, or buy the new generation resources and electricity needed to satisfy their energy demands, and pay for all new power delivery infrastructure upgrades to service their data centers.”“People think that if a data center goes in, their electricity prices are going to go up.”The move comes as tech companies scramble to quell growing opposition to data centers that require tremendous amounts of electricity to train and run generative AI models. Household electricity bills rose 13 percent nationally in 2025, according to a December report from advocacy group Climate Power. And data center electricity demand could double or triple by 2028, the Department of Energy estimates.According to the proclamation, the seven companies present at the event have “accepted the terms of the Ratepayer Protection Pledge” and that “the commitments embodied therein effectuate the national policy of the United States.” It adds, however, that the companies would still need to “voluntarily negotiate” agreements with utilities and state governments.The president said that the companies will be responsible for adding capacity to the grid “where possible.” He also said they would cover the costs of upgrading existing power infrastructure to meet growing electricity demand. Trump added that the companies should negotiate separate rate structures with utilities, an attempt at ensuring they pay a fair rate for all the extra pressure a data center puts on the grid. Companies would be on the hook for these costs even if data centers don’t wind up using all of the additional electricity generated. That’s a key measure that could address fears that local communities would be left holding the tab for new power plants and transmission lines that become stranded assets if hype around AI fizzles out and data center projects fall flat.Trump said that the tech giants would “use their infrastructure to contribute back up power to local grids during times of need.” Reducing how much power a data center uses when electricity demand peaks — during a severe winter storm or heatwave, for example — is a measure that could help prevent power outages during disasters. This year’s winter storms have raised concerns about how new data centers could further stress power grids and hike up electricity prices during disasters. Texas passed a law last year giving the local grid operator the authority to cut data centers’ energy use during an emergency. The pledge itself is more vague, saying that companies would “whenever possible, make available their backup generation resources at times of scarcity.”During the event, Gwynne Shotwell, President and COO of SpaceX (which recently announced it’s merging with xAI with the idea of pursuing plans to shoot data centers into space), said that xAI would develop a 1.2 gigawatt power plant as its supercomputer’s primary power source. The company would similarly develop as much power generation for “every additional data center,” Shotwell said. xAi also plans to expand its Megapack installation to provide backup power to Memphis, Tennessee and Southaven, Mississippi. The NAACP has already threatened to sue xAI twice over pollution from temporary gas turbines it has installed in Tennessee and Mississippi to power its data centers.The pledge also includes a commitment to hire from local communities where data centers are under construction. Meta announced today that it launched a pilot program in Ohio to train fiber technicians, including some who attended today’s event.
history.author: Justine Calma
history.titleZh: 七家科技巨头签署特朗普承诺，避免数据中心电价激增
history.descriptionZh:
为应对人工智能数据中心激增可能推高居民电价的担忧，美国七家顶尖科技公司与特朗普政府于今日共同签署了一项《费率支付者保护承诺》。谷歌、Meta、微软、Oracle、OpenAI、亚马逊及马斯克旗下xAI的负责人齐聚华盛顿，在总统特朗普主持的圆桌会议上正式作出这一承诺，旨在回应两党日益增长的关切——随着科技公司与政府大力建设新一代AI数据中心，电价上涨正成为严峻的社会问题。

特朗普在活动中坦言：“科技公司需要一些公关帮助，因为人们认为，只要数据中心入驻，他们的电费就会上涨。一些社区曾因此拒绝数据中心项目，但现在我认为情况将会逆转。”该承诺的核心在于要求企业为其激增的电力需求承担全部基础设施成本。根据公告，签署公司承诺将“建设、引入或购买满足其能源需求的新一代资源和电力，并支付所有为服务其数据中心而新建的电力输送基础设施升级费用”。

这一举措的背景是，用于训练和运行生成式AI模型的数据中心耗电量巨大，已引发多地社区的反对浪潮。倡导组织“气候力量”去年12月的报告显示，2025年美国家庭电费账单全国平均上涨了13%，而美国能源部估计，到2028年数据中心电力需求可能翻倍甚至增至三倍。

承诺书的关键条款包括：
1.  **成本内部化**：企业需自行承担电网扩容和基础设施升级费用，即使数据中心最终未完全使用所增发的电力。此举旨在防止AI热潮退去后，因数据中心项目停滞而导致新建电厂和输电线路成为“搁浅资产”，从而将成本转嫁给当地社区。
2.  **弹性用电与电网支持**：特朗普提出，科技巨头应“在可能的情况下，利用其基础设施在需求高峰时期向本地电网提供备用电力”。例如，在严冬风暴或热浪期间削减数据中心用电，有助于防止灾难性停电。承诺书更具体地表述为“尽可能在电力稀缺时期提供其备用发电资源”。此前，德州已通过法律授权电网运营商在紧急情况下切断数据中心供电。
3.  **独立费率谈判**：企业需与公用事业公司和州政府自愿协商单独的费率结构，以确保为其对电网造成的额外负荷支付公平费用。

活动中，SpaceX（已宣布与xAI合并并探索将数据中心送入太空的计划）总裁兼首席运营官格温·肖特韦尔透露，xAI将建设一座1.2吉瓦的发电厂作为其超级计算机的主要电源，并承诺为“每一个新增数据中心”配套开发相应规模的发电能力。此外，xAI计划扩展其Megapack储能装置，为田纳西州孟菲斯和密西西比州南黑文提供备用电源。值得注意的是，xAI因在田纳西和密西西比使用临时燃气轮机为数据中心供电造成污染，已两次受到美国全国有色人种协进会（NAACP）的法律诉讼威胁。

除电力承诺外，该协议还包括从数据中心建设所在地社区招募员工的条款。Meta今日宣布在俄亥俄州启动一项培训光纤技术员的试点项目，部分受训人员出席了本次活动。

这份承诺虽不具有法律强制力，但通过将企业责任与基础设施投资直接捆绑，试图在AI竞赛与民生负担间寻求平衡点，其具体实施效果将取决于后续企业与地方政府的谈判及监管跟进。

history.title: Taiwan expects power demand to increase by more than 5GW by 2030, enough to power nearly 4 million homes — rise in electricity consumption driven by semiconductor manufacturing and AI data center deployments
history.description: New semiconductor fabs and data centers are fueling Taiwan's growing power demand, with the island expected to require more than 5GW of new electricity sources and power infrastructure to keep up.
history.content: Copy link Facebook X Whatsapp Reddit Pinterest Flipboard Email Share this article 2 Join the conversation Follow us Add us as a preferred source on Google Newsletter Tom's Hardware Get the Tom's Hardware Newsletter Get Tom's Hardware's best news and in-depth reviews, straight to your inbox. You are now subscribed Your newsletter sign-up was successful An account already exists for this email address, please log in. Subscribe to our newsletter Taiwan is facing a new challenge in its goal to retain its advantage in AI and advanced semiconductor manufacturing — crucial for maintaining its silicon shield — as it grapples with a tightening power supply. Electricity demand is rising sharply as Taiwanese tech companies build semiconductor manufacturing facilities alongside AI data centers. According to Digitimes, both the AI and semiconductor industries could add about 5 gigawatts of demand — enough to power upwards of 3.75 million homes — by 2030, roughly a gigawatt of additional load per year through the end of the decade.Go deeper with TH Premium: AI and data centers (Image credit: Microsoft)Photonics and high-speed data movement is the next big AI bottleneckThe data center cooling state of playMassive AI data center buildouts are squeezing energy suppliesUltra Ethernet: The data center interconnection of tomorrowThis expansion, fueled by increasing global demand for the most advanced AI chips, is putting even more pressure on the island’s grid. Taipower chairperson Wen-sheng Tseng notes that each wafer fab needs around 200MW to operate, suggesting that the multiple facilities planned for and being built around the island will demand 5.3 to 5.4 gigawatts of electricity. This does not count the other plants and factories needed to support the operation of these factories, so it’s likely that the power demand will even be higher than projected. Nevertheless, four new electricity-generating gas units are set to come online this year, with an additional 5.2GW expected to enter trials soon. The company is also working to beef up Taiwan’s grid and enhance resilience plans, ensuring that the island’s power supply can keep up with the demand, particularly from science parks and industrial zones.Despite that, Taipower is still warning that demand is also increasing outside of these areas. Some fabs and servers are setting up facilities beyond these designated sectors, sometimes even requesting up to 20% of the power consumption of a science park for just a single structure. Because of this, Tseng says that both fabs and tech companies should be careful when selecting sites for their future infrastructure, ensuring that they’ll only be built in places where there is ample power supply to avoid bottlenecks in the future. You may like U.S. electricity grid stretches thin as data centers rush to turn on onsite generators Anthropic promises to pay for electricity price increases due to it's AI data centers Nvidia-backed trial shows AI data centers can flexibly adjust power use in near real time, with global implications for energy consumption Even though Taiwan’s power utility company is warning about power consumption, it seems to be on track to cover the future growth of the AI and semiconductor industry, provided that the current projections hold. This is a far cry from what the U.S. is experiencing, with the White House resorting to forcing big tech companies to promise that they will pay for the grid and power source upgrades needed to supply the massive amounts of electricity that AI data centers need. history.author: Jowi Morales
history.titleZh: 台湾2030年电力需求将激增逾5吉瓦，相当于近400万户家庭用电量——半导体制造与人工智能数据中心部署推动用电量增长
history.descriptionZh:
台湾作为全球人工智能与先进半导体制造的关键枢纽，正面临电力供应趋紧的新挑战，这直接关系到其维持技术领先地位与所谓的“硅盾”战略。随着科技公司加速建设晶圆厂和AI数据中心，岛内电力需求急剧攀升。据《电子时报》分析，到2030年，AI与半导体产业预计将新增约5吉瓦（GW）的用电需求，相当于375万户家庭的年用电量，即年均增加近1吉瓦负荷。

这一增长主要由全球对尖端AI芯片的旺盛需求驱动，给台湾电网带来持续压力。台电董事长曾文生指出，单座晶圆厂通常需要约200兆瓦（MW）的运营电力，而全岛规划及在建的多座工厂累计需求将达5.3至5.4吉瓦。若计入配套设施的能耗，实际电力需求可能更高。为此，台电计划今年投入四台新的燃气发电机组，并预计很快启动总容量5.2吉瓦的试运行，同时加强电网升级与韧性规划，重点保障科学园区及工业区的供电稳定。

然而，台电警告称，电力需求增长已超出传统高耗能区域。部分晶圆厂与服务器设施选择在非规划区建设，单个建筑的用电需求甚至可能达到科学园区总负荷的20%。曾文生因此建议企业未来选址时需审慎评估，确保所在地拥有充足的电力容量，以避免形成供应瓶颈。

尽管存在挑战，若当前预测准确，台湾似乎有望通过基础设施扩容满足AI与半导体产业的电力需求。这一情况与美国形成鲜明对比：美国政府正被迫要求科技巨头承诺自行承担电网升级费用，以支撑AI数据中心惊人的耗能。台湾的应对策略凸显了其将电力保障视为产业竞争力的核心要素，在推进技术主导地位的同时，也需在能源规划与区域发展间取得平衡。
